column2: — Lilichka appeared in the doorway.
— Well, tea...
— Miyechka felt a sudden irritation at the little saucepan, at the water that just wouldn't boil, and at her sister.
— Make me some too, — Lilichka sat down at the table and leaned against the wall.
Her high forehead was pale.
She seemed small, half-transparent and defenseless.
And that was what drew men to her so.
Miyechka silently took another mug.
The water hadn't yet had time to boil, but inside her everything was already boiling.
— Why don't you sleep with Lusya?
— she asked, pouring the tea.
— Meaning?
— Lilichka started.
— I'm asking why you don't sleep with Lusya, — Miyechka repeated.
— But Marta sleeps there.
— That's exactly why I'm asking: why should Marta be the one to sleep with this child?
— Listen, Solomiya, she wanted to herself.
— And so?
You do understand that this little girl is your responsibility?
She was left with you, not with me and not with Marta.
Lilichka just looked at her sister.
— What, is she in your way?
— It's not a question of whether she's in anyone's way, Lilya.
It just seems to me that you don't understand the scale of the situation.
— What exactly is it that I don't understand?
— Lilichka's voice was beginning to tremble.
— Ivan isn't going to take her back.
— He won't.
— Lusya is yours.
Not Marta's.
— Are you just irritated because your love drove off?
— You're winding me up on purpose, Lilya.
— You're just in a bad mood.
You don't have to make me tea.
— I'm talking to you about important things.
— You're not talking, you're lecturing me.
I didn't sign up for Lusya either, — said Lilichka, leaning forward.
— And are you ready to take her on yourself?
— And are you?
— I can.
If it's clearly decided, — retorted Miyechka and, grabbing the hot handle, yelped.
— Damn it, blast, — she said, pressing her burned finger to her mouth.
— Put it under water, — Lilichka exclaimed, jumping up.
— Oh, get away with you!
— Miyechka recoiled from her sister.
They switched to a loud whisper.
Marta, who had been sleeping badly lately anyway, heard the whispering and came into the kitchen.
Lilichka was crying.
Miyechka was holding her finger under the tap.
— What's going on here?
— asked Marta.
Her black, heavy hair was disheveled.
She was smoothing her eyebrow, the little hairs of which had also gone every which way.
Marta was pale and sleepy.
— Solomiya burned her finger, — said Lilichka.
— Aha.
And you feel sorry for her, so you're bawling?
— asked Marta.
— I'm crying because Solomiya says you shouldn't sleep with Lusya, because Lusya is my responsibility, and she's probably right…
Solomiya glanced at her sister.
Her irritation suddenly passed, and she started to make excuses:
— Listen, Lilya, I didn't mean...
— You meant exactly that!
— Lilichka cried out.
— You're right, Mia, it really is my responsibility, and I shouldn't have shifted it onto anyone else.
I'll call Greg, let him bring the little one, we'll take Lusya with us and go.
The summer's over, — Lilichka spoke quickly, without stopping, wiping away the tears that rolled down her cheeks all the way to her neck.
— What do you mean, you'll take Lusya?
— Marta raised her eyebrows.
— That's not what I meant, — said Miyechka.
— Lusya is not a suitcase, — said Marta.
— It's just, you understand, girls, we have to decide whose she'll be.
Who is ready to take Lusya as a daughter?
— asked Miyechka and looked at her sisters.
Both of them silently raised their hands.
Miyechka raised her hand too.
For a moment the three of them froze with their hands raised.
Marta, leaning her back against the doorframe, Lilichka with her knees pulled up under her T-shirt, sitting by the wall at the table, and Miyechka, holding her other hand under the cold water.
— Okay, — Marta broke through the trickle of water.
— Mia, come here.
Marta pulled a stool up to the table, sat down herself and tugged Solomiya by the hand toward the table.
The three of them sat in silence.
Then Marta took the matches that lay by the stove, used for lighting the gas when the wind tore down the power lines and there was no electricity.
She took three little matches out of the box, broke one of them in half and said:
— Whoever draws the short match, Lusya will be hers.
— What, do you want us to draw lots for the child with matches?
— asked Lilichka, wiping the tears from her neck.
— The short match is Lusya, — said Marta.
— Let me, — Miyechka took the matches from Marta's hands, turned away and arranged them between her fingers, and then said to Marta: — Draw.
Marta drew the short match.
While everyone else slept a few more hours and woke up closer to eleven, the grandmother sat alone at the kitchen table.
Across from her — Lusya, eating porridge made with milk.
She stirred the spoon in the bowl, licked it, then stirred again, while the grandmother sat leaning on the table, looking at the little girl, and said:
— Now Miyechka ate nicely as a child.
Or was that Marta?
For the grandmother all the children had by now gotten mixed up with the grandchildren and great-grandchildren.
Children's little hands, little fingers, sturdy little legs and thin little knees seemed to flicker before her eyes like a kaleidoscope.
She had already forgotten which of them was which, and out of habit spoke to Lusya the way she had spoken to all her children for the last half-century.
— We all wanted a girl so much, — the grandmother said to Lusya, scooping strawberry jam onto a spoon.
— Let me add some to your porridge.
— Granny, that's too sweet for her, — Lilichka was the first to wake up.
— And you want the child not to eat sweets?
Children need sweets in order to think.
And why are you all sleeping so long?
— We were guarding against the roof leaking.
That day not one of the sisters mentioned Marta's short match.
Everything went on as before.
Everyone played with Lusya.
Maria took up the pumpkins and walked carefully among them, scratching her legs on their stems, and pulled out the odd weed that had grown here and there.
For a while she felt needed here, these pumpkins gladdened her, and the field of weeds, which she considered her responsibility, stopped tormenting her.
She loved the greenery that grew exotically in marble pots and whose branches had to be trimmed and whose fallen leaves had to be swept from the floor.
The greenery that overran her life like weeds she did not love.

Maria wanted to send her daughters as far away as possible from this responsibility, away from these bushes and relentless windfalls, but they were drawn here by an invisible force, and she could do nothing about it.
How could she take up the fight against this green weave, when even Marta had been drawn here, Marta who had seemingly hidden safely five thousand kilometers away.
Lilichka was tense that day.
She was on the phone all the time, breaking up the children's fights from a distance, having long, exhausting conversations with her husband, and altogether looked tired.
— What's going on?
— Miyechka pressed.
— I told Greg about Lusya.
— And what did you say?
— Just how it is.
That Ivan brought her.
Greg was Lilichka's third husband.
They had met in Mexico.
Lilichka had gone to look at Frida — a heavy-browed and tender creature.
— How could she live with such a despot?
— she whispered, looking over the correspondence with Diego.
— And how could I live with Ivan and Aiven?
— she said to herself.
It was there that Greg found the bewildered and unhappy Lilichka.
Greg's mother was Spanish.
Dark-skinned, loud, emotional.
Greg by temperament resembled his father — a quiet, fair-haired Irishman.
He was fair, with black brows and a black beard.
Greg wanted to protect Lilichka from the Mexicans, from the heat and from boredom.
Lilichka's attempt to be alone with herself on her travels had fallen through.
Greg too was at a crossroads then.
He thought he'd never want a family again.
It seemed he wanted a motorcycle and a trip around the world.
He bought a shiny black motorcycle and rode about two hundred kilometers on it.

He only managed to cross the border between the United States and Mexico, and waiting for him there already was the bewildered Lilichka with her little bird's head and half-transparent skin, which she constantly hid from the sun.
Greg sold the motorcycle within two weeks.
— Lili, — Greg would say, — you bring me back to life.
Lilichka didn't like that hackneyed phrase, but she understood that he was speaking sincerely.

Besides, he had handsome legs, rugged brows and a quiet, gentle temperament 90% of the time, until now and then his Spanish mother Natalia (whose parents had evidently been communists) would wake up in him, the one everyone simply called Nati, with a hard 't.'
Today, when Lilichka told Greg about Lusya, while he was just then frying toast for the children with his mother, Nati woke up in him.
He hung up, and then called back another eight times.
— Why did you tell him everything so dramatically?
— asked Miyechka.
— I'm not going to keep protecting him from hysteria all the time.
— You could have been gentler and avoided the sharp edges.
— I'm sick to death of always avoiding them.
He and Nati will shout it out there now and calm down.
Their shared son Roberto was white as milk.
Nati, his Spanish grandmother, couldn't come to terms with the fact that this child, even just to be sent to the local shop for tomatoes, had to be smeared from head to toe with 50+ sunscreen.
At first she protested.
But when the skin on little Roberto's ears peeled right after a trip to the shop, Nati gave in.
— What kind of child is this, — she would wail all the time, slathering Roberto from head to toe, — who can't go out in the sun?
Roberto turned out thin, like Lilichka, with almost transparent hair, like his father's.

Of the Spanish in him there was only the name, which had been a concession to Natalia, when, peering into the newborn's red, browless little face, she still nursed hopes that he would grow up dark-skinned, like her father, grandfather and brothers.
— This hair, — Nati would say, — looks more like goose down than human locks.
As if this Irish son with his straw-colored hair weren't enough, now I have a grandson with hair like a young gosling's.
Even earlier, on seeing a photograph of Lilichka, Nati had said to Greg: 'Are there really not enough women in all of Spain?
Fine, not in all of Spain, since what do you want with pale ones from the north, but couldn't you have chosen someone from Seville, or at least from Málaga?
Couldn't you have chosen someone at least a little like your own mother?'
Greg laughed.

But when the pregnant Lilichka first arrived with Marko, her son, at their small Spanish house, to which Greg's mother had decided to return for her retirement, Nati threw up her hands: 'This isn't Marko!
This is Fernando!'
Marko later made up to her for her own grandson Roberto.
Marko, Lilichka's son with Ivan, had pitch-black hair.
He was stocky, with slightly bowed legs and dark skin.
Nati embraced the child and took the boy as her own.
And Greg had an annual duty to spend a few weeks with his mother.
He spoke of it as a duty, but in reality it seemed to Lilichka that he wanted it.
That he loved his mother's dishes, loved being with her.
At first Lilichka thought that maybe she ought to be jealous of Greg's mother, until Miyechka said to her:
— Would it be better if he demanded all that from you?
Let him go and have his fill of her sauces.
— You're right, Mia, — Lilichka would reply.
— After Spain he doesn't eat fish for a year.
Now Lilichka, Miyechka and the grandmother sat at the table.
The grandmother was just passing from mourning the relatives to their shortcomings, when Greg called again and said that he and his mother would take Lusya in.
— Listen, — Lilichka said into the phone, — Marta has already drawn the short match…
— What?
— Greg shouted into the receiver.
— What match?
— Well, you know, that's how we do it…
— How do you do it?
You choose children with matches?
